1	---
2	name: graphify
3	description: "any input (code, docs, papers, images) - knowledge graph - clustered communities - HTML + JSON + audit report"
4	trigger: /graphify
5	---
6	
7	# /graphify
8	
9	Turn any folder of files into a navigable knowledge graph with community detection, an honest audit trail, and three outputs: interactive HTML, GraphRAG-ready JSON, and a plain-language GRAPH_REPORT.md.
10	
11	## Usage
12	
13	```
14	/graphify                                             # full pipeline on current directory → Obsidian vault
15	/graphify <path>                                      # full pipeline on specific path
16	/graphify https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>           # clone repo then run full pipeline on it
17	/graphify https://github.com/<owner>/<repo> --branch <branch>  # clone a specific branch
18	/graphify <url1> <url2> ...                           # clone multiple repos, build each, merge into one cross-repo graph
19	/graphify <path> --mode deep                          # thorough extraction, richer INFERRED edges
20	/graphify <path> --update                             # incremental - re-extract only new/changed files
21	/graphify <path> --directed                            # build directed graph (preserves edge direction: source→target)
22	/graphify <path> --whisper-model medium                # use a larger Whisper model for better transcription accuracy
23	/graphify <path> --cluster-only                       # rerun clustering on existing graph
24	/graphify <path> --no-viz                             # skip visualization, just report + JSON
25	/graphify <path> --html                               # (HTML is generated by default - this flag is a no-op)
26	/graphify <path> --svg                                # also export graph.svg (embeds in Notion, GitHub)
27	/graphify <path> --graphml                            # export graph.graphml (Gephi, yEd)
28	/graphify <path> --neo4j                              # generate graphify-out/cypher.txt for Neo4j
29	/graphify <path> --neo4j-push bolt://localhost:7687   # push directly to Neo4j
30	/graphify <path> --mcp                                # start MCP stdio server for agent access
31	/graphify <path> --watch                              # watch folder, auto-rebuild on code changes (no LLM needed)
32	/graphify <path> --wiki                               # build agent-crawlable wiki (index.md + one article per community)
33	/graphify <path> --obsidian --obsidian-dir ~/vaults/my-project  # write vault to custom path (e.g. existing vault)
34	/graphify add <url>                                   # fetch URL, save to ./raw, update graph
35	/graphify add <url> --author "Name"                   # tag who wrote it
36	/graphify add <url> --contributor "Name"              # tag who added it to the corpus
37	/graphify query "<question>"                          # BFS traversal - broad context
38	/graphify query "<question>" --dfs                    # DFS - trace a specific path
39	/graphify query "<question>" --budget 1500            # cap answer at N tokens
40	/graphify path "AuthModule" "Database"                # shortest path between two concepts
41	/graphify explain "SwinTransformer"                   # plain-language explanation of a node
42	```
43	
44	## What graphify is for
45	
46	graphify is built around Andrej Karpathy's /raw folder workflow: drop anything into a folder - papers, tweets, screenshots, code, notes - and get a structured knowledge graph that shows you what you didn't know was connected.
47	
48	Three things it does that Claude alone cannot:
49	1. **Persistent graph** - relationships are stored in `graphify-out/graph.json` and survive across sessions. Ask questions weeks later without re-reading everything.
50	2. **Honest audit trail** - every edge is tagged EXTRACTED, INFERRED, or AMBIGUOUS. You know what was found vs invented.
51	3. **Cross-document surprise** - community detection finds connections between concepts in different files that you would never think to ask about directly.
52	
53	Use it for:
54	- A codebase you're new to (understand architecture before touching anything)
55	- A reading list (papers + tweets + notes → one navigable graph)
56	- A research corpus (citation graph + concept graph in one)
57	- Your personal /raw folder (drop everything in, let it grow, query it)
58	
59	## What You Must Do When Invoked
60	
61	If no path was given, use `.` (current directory). Do not ask the user for a path.
62	
63	If the path argument starts with `https://github.com/` or `http://github.com/`, treat it as a GitHub URL — run Step 0 before anything else, then continue with the resolved local path.
64	
65	Follow these steps in order. Do not skip steps.
66	
67	### Step 0 - Clone GitHub repo(s) (only if a GitHub URL was given)
68	
69	**Single repo:**
70	```bash
71	LOCAL_PATH=$(graphify clone <github-url> [--branch <branch>])
72	# Use LOCAL_PATH as the target for all subsequent steps
73	```
74	
75	**Multiple repos (cross-repo graph):**
76	```bash
77	# Clone each repo, run the full pipeline on each, then merge
78	graphify clone <url1>   # → ~/.graphify/repos/<owner1>/<repo1>
79	graphify clone <url2>   # → ~/.graphify/repos/<owner2>/<repo2>
80	# Run /graphify on each local path to produce their graph.json files
81	# Then merge:
82	graphify merge-graphs \
83	  ~/.graphify/repos/<owner1>/<repo1>/graphify-out/graph.json \
84	  ~/.graphify/repos/<owner2>/<repo2>/graphify-out/graph.json \
85	  --out graphify-out/cross-repo-graph.json
86	```
87	
88	Graphify clones into `~/.graphify/repos/<owner>/<repo>` and reuses existing clones on repeat runs. Each node in the merged graph carries a `repo` attribute so you can filter by origin.
89	
90	### Step 1 - Ensure graphify is installed
91	
92	```bash
93	# Detect the correct Python interpreter (handles uv tool, pipx, venv, system installs)
94	PYTHON=""
95	GRAPHIFY_BIN=$(which graphify 2>/dev/null)
96	# 1. uv tool installs — most reliable on modern Mac/Linux
97	if [ -z "$PYTHON" ] && command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1; then
98	    _UV_PY=$(uv tool run graphifyy python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)" 2>/dev/null)
99	    if [ -n "$_UV_PY" ]; then PYTHON="$_UV_PY"; fi
100	fi
101	# 2. Read shebang from graphify binary (pipx and direct pip installs)
102	if [ -z "$PYTHON" ] && [ -n "$GRAPHIFY_BIN" ]; then
103	    _SHEBANG=$(head -1 "$GRAPHIFY_BIN" | tr -d '#!')
104	    case "$_SHEBANG" in
105	        *[!a-zA-Z0-9/_.-]*) ;;
106	        *) "$_SHEBANG" -c "import graphify" 2>/dev/null && PYTHON="$_SHEBANG" ;;
107	    esac
108	fi
109	# 3. Fall back to python3
110	if [ -z "$PYTHON" ]; then PYTHON="python3"; fi
111	"$PYTHON" -c "import graphify" 2>/dev/null || "$PYTHON" -m pip install graphifyy -q 2>/dev/null || "$PYTHON" -m pip install graphifyy -q --break-system-packages 2>&1 | tail -3
112	# Write interpreter path for all subsequent steps (persists across invocations)
113	mkdir -p graphify-out
114	"$PYTHON" -c "import sys; open('graphify-out/.graphify_python', 'w').write(sys.executable)"
115	```
116	
117	If the import succeeds, print nothing and move straight to Step 2.
118	
119	**In every subsequent bash block, replace `python3` with `$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python)` to use the correct interpreter.**
120	
121	### Step 2 - Detect files
122	
123	```bash
124	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
125	import json
126	from graphify.detect import detect
127	from pathlib import Path
128	result = detect(Path('INPUT_PATH'))
129	print(json.dumps(result))
130	" > graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json
131	```
132	
133	Replace INPUT_PATH with the actual path the user provided. Do NOT cat or print the JSON - read it silently and present a clean summary instead:
134	
135	```
136	Corpus: X files · ~Y words
137	  code:     N files (.py .ts .go ...)
138	  docs:     N files (.md .txt ...)
139	  papers:   N files (.pdf ...)
140	  images:   N files
141	  video:    N files (.mp4 .mp3 ...)
142	```
143	
144	Omit any category with 0 files from the summary.
145	
146	Then act on it:
147	- If `total_files` is 0: stop with "No supported files found in [path]."
148	- If `skipped_sensitive` is non-empty: mention file count skipped, not the file names.
149	- If `total_words` > 2,000,000 OR `total_files` > 200: show the warning and the top 5 subdirectories by file count, then ask which subfolder to run on. Wait for the user's answer before proceeding.
150	- Otherwise: proceed directly to Step 2.5 if video files were detected, or Step 3 if not.
151	
152	### Step 2.5 - Transcribe video / audio files (only if video files detected)
153	
154	Skip this step entirely if `detect` returned zero `video` files.
155	
156	Video and audio files cannot be read directly. Transcribe them to text first, then treat the transcripts as doc files in Step 3.
157	
158	**Strategy:** Read the god nodes from `graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json` (or the analysis file if it exists from a previous run). You are already a language model — write a one-sentence domain hint yourself from those labels. Then pass it to Whisper as the initial prompt. No separate API call needed.
159	
160	**However**, if the corpus has *only* video files and no other docs/code, use the generic fallback prompt: `"Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks."`
161	
162	**Step 1 - Write the Whisper prompt yourself.**
163	
164	Read the top god node labels from detect output or analysis, then compose a short domain hint sentence, for example:
165	
166	- Labels: `transformer, attention, encoder, decoder` → `"Machine learning research on transformer architectures and attention mechanisms. Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks."`
167	- Labels: `kubernetes, deployment, pod, helm` → `"DevOps discussion about Kubernetes deployments and Helm charts. Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks."`
168	
169	Set it as `WHISPER_PROMPT` to use in the next command.
170	
171	**Step 2 - Transcribe:**
172	
173	```bash
174	GRAPHIFY_WHISPER_MODEL=base  # or whatever --whisper-model the user passed
175	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
176	import json, os
177	from pathlib import Path
178	from graphify.transcribe import transcribe_all
179	
180	detect = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())
181	video_files = detect.get('files', {}).get('video', [])
182	prompt = os.environ.get('GRAPHIFY_WHISPER_PROMPT', 'Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks.')
183	
184	transcript_paths = transcribe_all(video_files, initial_prompt=prompt)
185	print(json.dumps(transcript_paths))
186	" > graphify-out/.graphify_transcripts.json
187	```
188	
189	After transcription:
190	- Read the transcript paths from `graphify-out/.graphify_transcripts.json`
191	- Add them to the docs list before dispatching semantic subagents in Step 3B
192	- Print how many transcripts were created: `Transcribed N video file(s) -> treating as docs`
193	- If transcription fails for a file, print a warning and continue with the rest
194	
195	**Whisper model:** Default is `base`. If the user passed `--whisper-model <name>`, set `GRAPHIFY_WHISPER_MODEL=<name>` in the environment before running the command above.
196	
197	### Step 3 - Extract entities and relationships
198	
199	**Before starting:** note whether `--mode deep` was given. You must pass `DEEP_MODE=true` to every subagent in Step B2 if it was. Track this from the original invocation - do not lose it.
200	
201	This step has two parts: **structural extraction** (deterministic, free) and **semantic extraction** (Claude, costs tokens).
202	
203	**Run Part A (AST) and Part B (semantic) in parallel. Dispatch all semantic subagents AND start AST extraction in the same message. Both can run simultaneously since they operate on different file types. Merge results in Part C as before.**
204	
205	Note: Parallelizing AST + semantic saves 5-15s on large corpora. AST is deterministic and fast; start it while subagents are processing docs/papers.
206	
207	#### Part A - Structural extraction for code files
208	
209	For any code files detected, run AST extraction in parallel with Part B subagents:
210	
211	```bash
212	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
213	import sys, json
214	from graphify.extract import collect_files, extract
215	from pathlib import Path
216	import json
217	
218	code_files = []
219	detect = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())
220	for f in detect.get('files', {}).get('code', []):
221	    code_files.extend(collect_files(Path(f)) if Path(f).is_dir() else [Path(f)])
222	
223	if code_files:
224	    result = extract(code_files, cache_root=Path('.'))
225	    Path('graphify-out/.graphify_ast.json').write_text(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
226	    print(f'AST: {len(result[\"nodes\"])} nodes, {len(result[\"edges\"])} edges')
227	else:
228	    Path('graphify-out/.graphify_ast.json').write_text(json.dumps({'nodes':[],'edges':[],'input_tokens':0,'output_tokens':0}))
229	    print('No code files - skipping AST extraction')
230	"
231	```
232	
233	#### Part B - Semantic extraction (parallel subagents)
234	
235	**Fast path:** If detection found zero docs, papers, and images (code-only corpus), skip Part B entirely and go straight to Part C. AST handles code - there is nothing for semantic subagents to do.
236	
237	**MANDATORY: You MUST use the Agent tool here. Reading files yourself one-by-one is forbidden - it is 5-10x slower. If you do not use the Agent tool you are doing this wrong.**
238	
239	Before dispatching subagents, print a timing estimate:
240	- Load `total_words` and file counts from `graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json`
241	- Estimate agents needed: `ceil(uncached_non_code_files / 22)` (chunk size is 20-25)
242	- Estimate time: ~45s per agent batch (they run in parallel, so total ≈ 45s × ceil(agents/parallel_limit))
243	- Print: "Semantic extraction: ~N files → X agents, estimated ~Ys"
244	
245	**Step B0 - Check extraction cache first**
246	
247	Before dispatching any subagents, check which files already have cached extraction results:
248	
249	```bash
250	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
251	import json
252	from graphify.cache import check_semantic_cache
253	from pathlib import Path
254	
255	detect = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())
256	all_files = [f for files in detect['files'].values() for f in files]
257	
258	cached_nodes, cached_edges, cached_hyperedges, uncached = check_semantic_cache(all_files)
259	
260	if cached_nodes or cached_edges or cached_hyperedges:
261	    Path('graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json').write_text(json.dumps({'nodes': cached_nodes, 'edges': cached_edges, 'hyperedges': cached_hyperedges}))
262	Path('graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt').write_text('\n'.join(uncached))
263	print(f'Cache: {len(all_files)-len(uncached)} files hit, {len(uncached)} files need extraction')
264	"
265	```
266	
267	Only dispatch subagents for files listed in `graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt`. If all files are cached, skip to Part C directly.
268	
269	**Step B1 - Split into chunks**
270	
271	Load files from `graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt`. Split into chunks of 20-25 files each. Each image gets its own chunk (vision needs separate context). When splitting, group files from the same directory together so related artifacts land in the same chunk and cross-file relationships are more likely to be extracted.
272	
273	**Step B2 - Dispatch ALL subagents in a single message**
274	
275	Call the Agent tool multiple times IN THE SAME RESPONSE - one call per chunk. This is the only way they run in parallel. If you make one Agent call, wait, then make another, you are doing it sequentially and defeating the purpose.
276	
277	**IMPORTANT - subagent type:** Always use `subagent_type="general-purpose"`. Do NOT use `Explore` - it is read-only and cannot write chunk files to disk, which silently drops extraction results. General-purpose has Write and Bash access which the subagent needs.
278	
279	Concrete example for 3 chunks:
280	```
281	[Agent tool call 1: files 1-15, subagent_type="general-purpose"]
282	[Agent tool call 2: files 16-30, subagent_type="general-purpose"]
283	[Agent tool call 3: files 31-45, subagent_type="general-purpose"]
284	```
285	All three in one message. Not three separate messages.
286	
287	Each subagent receives this exact prompt (substitute FILE_LIST, CHUNK_NUM, TOTAL_CHUNKS, and DEEP_MODE):
288	
289	```
290	You are a graphify extraction subagent. Read the files listed and extract a knowledge graph fragment.
291	Output ONLY valid JSON matching the schema below - no explanation, no markdown fences, no preamble.
292	
293	Files (chunk CHUNK_NUM of TOTAL_CHUNKS):
294	FILE_LIST
295	
296	Rules:
297	- EXTRACTED: relationship explicit in source (import, call, citation, "see §3.2")
298	- INFERRED: reasonable inference (shared data structure, implied dependency)
299	- AMBIGUOUS: uncertain - flag for review, do not omit
300	
301	Code files: focus on semantic edges AST cannot find (call relationships, shared data, arch patterns).
302	  Do not re-extract imports - AST already has those.
303	Doc/paper files: extract named concepts, entities, citations. Also extract rationale — sections that explain WHY a decision was made, trade-offs chosen, or design intent. These become nodes with `rationale_for` edges pointing to the concept they explain.
304	Image files: use vision to understand what the image IS - do not just OCR.
305	  UI screenshot: layout patterns, design decisions, key elements, purpose.
306	  Chart: metric, trend/insight, data source.
307	  Tweet/post: claim as node, author, concepts mentioned.
308	  Diagram: components and connections.
309	  Research figure: what it demonstrates, method, result.
310	  Handwritten/whiteboard: ideas and arrows, mark uncertain readings AMBIGUOUS.
311	
312	DEEP_MODE (if --mode deep was given): be aggressive with INFERRED edges - indirect deps,
313	  shared assumptions, latent couplings. Mark uncertain ones AMBIGUOUS instead of omitting.
314	
315	Semantic similarity: if two concepts in this chunk solve the same problem or represent the same idea without any structural link (no import, no call, no citation), add a `semantically_similar_to` edge marked INFERRED with a confidence_score reflecting how similar they are (0.6-0.95). Examples:
316	- Two functions that both validate user input but never call each other
317	- A class in code and a concept in a paper that describe the same algorithm
318	- Two error types that handle the same failure mode differently
319	Only add these when the similarity is genuinely non-obvious and cross-cutting. Do not add them for trivially similar things.
320	
321	Hyperedges: if 3 or more nodes clearly participate together in a shared concept, flow, or pattern that is not captured by pairwise edges alone, add a hyperedge to a top-level `hyperedges` array. Examples:
322	- All classes that implement a common protocol or interface
323	- All functions in an authentication flow (even if they don't all call each other)
324	- All concepts from a paper section that form one coherent idea
325	Use sparingly — only when the group relationship adds information beyond the pairwise edges. Maximum 3 hyperedges per chunk.
326	
327	If a file has YAML frontmatter (--- ... ---), copy source_url, captured_at, author,
328	  contributor onto every node from that file.
329	
330	confidence_score is REQUIRED on every edge - never omit it, never use 0.5 as a default:
331	- EXTRACTED edges: confidence_score = 1.0 always
332	- INFERRED edges: reason about each edge individually.
333	  Direct structural evidence (shared data structure, clear dependency): 0.8-0.9.
334	  Reasonable inference with some uncertainty: 0.6-0.7.
335	  Weak or speculative: 0.4-0.5. Most edges should be 0.6-0.9, not 0.5.
336	- AMBIGUOUS edges: 0.1-0.3
337	
338	Node ID format: lowercase, only `[a-z0-9_]`, no dots or slashes. Format: `{stem}_{entity}` where stem is the filename without extension and entity is the symbol name, both normalized (lowercase, non-alphanumeric chars replaced with `_`). Example: `src/auth/session.py` + `ValidateToken` → `session_validatetoken`. This must match the ID the AST extractor generates so cross-references between code and semantic nodes connect correctly. CRITICAL: never append chunk numbers, sequence numbers, or any suffix to an ID (no `_c1`, `_c2`, `_chunk2`, etc.). IDs must be deterministic from the label alone — the same entity must always produce the same ID regardless of which chunk processes it.
339	
340	Output exactly this JSON (no other text):
341	{"nodes":[{"id":"session_validatetoken","label":"Human Readable Name","file_type":"code|document|paper|image","source_file":"relative/path","source_location":null,"source_url":null,"captured_at":null,"author":null,"contributor":null}],"edges":[{"source":"node_id","target":"node_id","relation":"calls|implements|references|cites|conceptually_related_to|shares_data_with|semantically_similar_to|rationale_for","confidence":"EXTRACTED|INFERRED|AMBIGUOUS","confidence_score":1.0,"source_file":"relative/path","source_location":null,"weight":1.0}],"hyperedges":[{"id":"snake_case_id","label":"Human Readable Label","nodes":["node_id1","node_id2","node_id3"],"relation":"participate_in|implement|form","confidence":"EXTRACTED|INFERRED","confidence_score":0.75,"source_file":"relative/path"}],"input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":0}
342	```
343	
344	**Step B3 - Collect, cache, and merge**
345	
346	Wait for all subagents. For each result:
347	- Check that `graphify-out/.graphify_chunk_NN.json` exists on disk — this is the success signal
348	- If the file exists and contains valid JSON with `nodes` and `edges`, include it and save to cache
349	- If the file is missing, the subagent was likely dispatched as read-only (Explore type) — print a warning: "chunk N missing from disk — subagent may have been read-only. Re-run with general-purpose agent." Do not silently skip.
350	- If a subagent failed or returned invalid JSON, print a warning and skip that chunk - do not abort
351	
352	If more than half the chunks failed or are missing, stop and tell the user to re-run and ensure `subagent_type="general-purpose"` is used.
353	
354	Save new results to cache:
355	```bash
356	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
357	import json
358	from graphify.cache import save_semantic_cache
359	from pathlib import Path
360	
361	new = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').exists() else {'nodes':[],'edges':[],'hyperedges':[]}
362	saved = save_semantic_cache(new.get('nodes', []), new.get('edges', []), new.get('hyperedges', []))
363	print(f'Cached {saved} files')
364	"
365	```
366	
367	Merge cached + new results into `graphify-out/.graphify_semantic.json`:
368	```bash
369	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
370	import json
371	from pathlib import Path
372	
373	cached = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json').exists() else {'nodes':[],'edges':[],'hyperedges':[]}
374	new = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').exists() else {'nodes':[],'edges':[],'hyperedges':[]}
375	
376	all_nodes = cached['nodes'] + new.get('nodes', [])
377	all_edges = cached['edges'] + new.get('edges', [])
378	all_hyperedges = cached.get('hyperedges', []) + new.get('hyperedges', [])
379	seen = set()
380	deduped = []
381	for n in all_nodes:
382	    if n['id'] not in seen:
383	        seen.add(n['id'])
384	        deduped.append(n)
385	
386	merged = {
387	    'nodes': deduped,
388	    'edges': all_edges,
389	    'hyperedges': all_hyperedges,
390	    'input_tokens': new.get('input_tokens', 0),
391	    'output_tokens': new.get('output_tokens', 0),
392	}
393	Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic.json').write_text(json.dumps(merged, indent=2))
394	print(f'Extraction complete - {len(deduped)} nodes, {len(all_edges)} edges ({len(cached[\"nodes\"])} from cache, {len(new.get(\"nodes\",[]))} new)')
395	"
396	```
397	Clean up temp files: `rm -f graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json`
398	
399	#### Part C - Merge AST + semantic into final extraction
400	
401	```bash
402	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
403	import sys, json
404	from pathlib import Path
405	
406	ast = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_ast.json').read_text())
407	sem = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic.json').read_text())
408	
409	# Merge: AST nodes first, semantic nodes deduplicated by id
410	seen = {n['id'] for n in ast['nodes']}
411	merged_nodes = list(ast['nodes'])
412	for n in sem['nodes']:
413	    if n['id'] not in seen:
414	        merged_nodes.append(n)
415	        seen.add(n['id'])
416	
417	merged_edges = ast['edges'] + sem['edges']
418	merged_hyperedges = sem.get('hyperedges', [])
419	merged = {
420	    'nodes': merged_nodes,
421	    'edges': merged_edges,
422	    'hyperedges': merged_hyperedges,
423	    'input_tokens': sem.get('input_tokens', 0),
424	    'output_tokens': sem.get('output_tokens', 0),
425	}
426	Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').write_text(json.dumps(merged, indent=2))
427	total = len(merged_nodes)
428	edges = len(merged_edges)
429	print(f'Merged: {total} nodes, {edges} edges ({len(ast[\"nodes\"])} AST + {len(sem[\"nodes\"])} semantic)')
430	"
431	```

568	import sys, json
569	from graphify.build import build_from_json
570	from graphify.export import to_html
571	from pathlib import Path
572	
573	extraction = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').read_text())
574	analysis   = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_analysis.json').read_text())
575	labels_raw = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_labels.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_labels.json').exists() else {}

579	labels = {int(k): v for k, v in labels_raw.items()}
580	
581	NODE_LIMIT = 5000
582	if G.number_of_nodes() > NODE_LIMIT:
583	    from collections import Counter
584	    print(f'Graph has {G.number_of_nodes()} nodes (above {NODE_LIMIT} limit). Building aggregated community view...')
585	    node_to_community = {nid: cid for cid, members in communities.items() for nid in members}
586	    import networkx as nx_meta
587	    meta = nx_meta.Graph()
588	    for cid, members in communities.items():
589	        meta.add_node(str(cid), label=labels.get(cid, f'Community {cid}'))
590	    edge_counts = Counter()
591	    for u, v in G.edges():
592	        cu, cv = node_to_community.get(u), node_to_community.get(v)
593	        if cu is not None and cv is not None and cu != cv:
594	            edge_counts[(min(cu, cv), max(cu, cv))] += 1
595	    for (cu, cv), w in edge_counts.items():
596	        meta.add_edge(str(cu), str(cv), weight=w, relation=f'{w} cross-community edges', confidence='AGGREGATED')
597	    if meta.number_of_nodes() > 1:
598	        meta_communities = {cid: [str(cid)] for cid in communities}
599	        member_counts = {cid: len(members) for cid, members in communities.items()}
600	        to_html(meta, meta_communities, 'graphify-out/graph.html', community_labels=labels or None, member_counts=member_counts)
601	        print(f'graph.html written (aggregated: {meta.number_of_nodes()} community nodes, {meta.number_of_edges()} cross-community edges)')
602	        print('Tip: run with --obsidian for full node-level detail.')
603	    else:
604	        print('Single community — aggregated view not useful. Skipping graph.html.')
605	else:
606	    to_html(G, communities, 'graphify-out/graph.html', community_labels=labels or None)
607	    print('graph.html written - open in any browser, no server needed')
608	"

904	# Load existing graph
905	existing_data = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/graph.json').read_text())
906	G_existing = json_graph.node_link_graph(existing_data, edges='links')
907	
908	# Load new extraction
909	new_extraction = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').read_text())
910	G_new = build_from_json(new_extraction)
911	
912	# Prune nodes from deleted files
913	incremental = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_incremental.json').read_text())
914	deleted = set(incremental.get('deleted_files', []))
915	if deleted:
916	    to_remove = [n for n, d in G_existing.nodes(data=True) if d.get('source_file') in deleted]
917	    G_existing.remove_nodes_from(to_remove)
918	    print(f'Pruned {len(to_remove)} ghost nodes from {len(deleted)} deleted file(s)')
919	
920	# Merge: new nodes/edges into existing graph
921	G_existing.update(G_new)
922	print(f'Merged: {G_existing.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {G_existing.number_of_edges()} edges')
923	
924	# Write merged result back to .graphify_extract.json so Step 4 sees the full graph
925	merged_out = {
926	    'nodes': [{'id': n, **d} for n, d in G_existing.nodes(data=True)],
927	    'edges': [{'source': u, 'target': v, **d} for u, v, d in G_existing.edges(data=True)],
928	    'hyperedges': new_extraction.get('hyperedges', []),
929	    'input_tokens': new_extraction.get('input_tokens', 0),
930	    'output_tokens': new_extraction.get('output_tokens', 0),
931	}
932	Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').write_text(json.dumps(merged_out))
933	print(f'[graphify update] Merged extraction written ({len(merged_out[\"nodes\"])} nodes, {len(merged_out[\"edges\"])} edges)')
934	" 
935	```
936	
937	Then run Steps 4–8 on the merged graph as normal.
938	
939	After Step 4, show the graph diff:
940	
941	```bash
942	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
943	import json
944	from graphify.analyze import graph_diff
945	from graphify.build import build_from_json
946	from networkx.readwrite import json_graph
947	import networkx as nx
948	from pathlib import Path
949	
950	# Load old graph (before update) from backup written before merge
951	old_data = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_old.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_old.json').exists() else None
952	new_extract = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').read_text())
953	G_new = build_from_json(new_extract)
954	
955	if old_data:
956	    G_old = json_graph.node_link_graph(old_data, edges='links')
957	    diff = graph_diff(G_old, G_new)
958	    print(diff['summary'])
959	    if diff['new_nodes']:
960	        print('New nodes:', ', '.join(n['label'] for n in diff['new_nodes'][:5]))
961	    if diff['new_edges']:
962	        print('New edges:', len(diff['new_edges']))
963	"
964	```
965	
966	Before the merge step, save the old graph: `cp graphify-out/graph.json graphify-out/.graphify_old.json`
967	Clean up after: `rm -f graphify-out/.graphify_old.json`
968	
969	---
970	
971	## For --cluster-only
972	
973	Skip Steps 1–3. Load the existing graph from `graphify-out/graph.json` and re-run clustering:
974	
975	```bash
976	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
977	import sys, json
978	from graphify.cluster import cluster, score_all
979	from graphify.analyze import god_nodes, surprising_connections
980	from graphify.report import generate
981	from graphify.export import to_json
982	from networkx.readwrite import json_graph
983	import networkx as nx
984	from pathlib import Path
985	
986	data = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/graph.json').read_text())
987	G = json_graph.node_link_graph(data, edges='links')
988	
989	detection = {'total_files': 0, 'total_words': 99999, 'needs_graph': True, 'warning': None,
990	             'files': {'code': [], 'document': [], 'paper': []}}
991	tokens = {'input': 0, 'output': 0}
992	
993	communities = cluster(G)
994	cohesion = score_all(G, communities)
995	gods = god_nodes(G)
996	surprises = surprising_connections(G, communities)
997	labels = {cid: 'Community ' + str(cid) for cid in communities}
998	
999	report = generate(G, communities, cohesion, labels, gods, surprises, detection, tokens, '.')
1000	Path('graphify-out/GRAPH_REPORT.md').write_text(report)
1001	to_json(G, communities, 'graphify-out/graph.json')
1002	
1003	analysis = {
1004	    'communities': {str(k): v for k, v in communities.items()},
1005	    'cohesion': {str(k): v for k, v in cohesion.items()},
1006	    'gods': gods,
1007	    'surprises': surprises,
1008	}
1009	Path('graphify-out/.graphify_analysis.json').write_text(json.dumps(analysis, indent=2))
1010	print(f'Re-clustered: {len(communities)} communities')
1011	"
1012	```
1013	
1014	Then run Steps 5–9 as normal (label communities, generate viz, benchmark, clean up, report).
1015	
1016	---
1017	
1018	## For /graphify query
1019	
1020	Two traversal modes - choose based on the question:
1021	
1022	| Mode | Flag | Best for |
1023	|------|------|----------|
1024	| BFS (default) | _(none)_ | "What is X connected to?" - broad context, nearest neighbors first |
1025	| DFS | `--dfs` | "How does X reach Y?" - trace a specific chain or dependency path |
1026	
1027	First check the graph exists:
1028	```bash
1029	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1030	from pathlib import Path
1031	if not Path('graphify-out/graph.json').exists():
1032	    print('ERROR: No graph found. Run /graphify <path> first to build the graph.')
1033	    raise SystemExit(1)
1034	"
1035	```
1036	If it fails, stop and tell the user to run `/graphify <path>` first.
1037	
1038	Load `graphify-out/graph.json`, then:
1039	
1040	1. Find the 1-3 nodes whose label best matches key terms in the question.
1041	2. Run the appropriate traversal from each starting node.
1042	3. Read the subgraph - node labels, edge relations, confidence tags, source locations.
1043	4. Answer using **only** what the graph contains. Quote `source_location` when citing a specific fact.
1044	5. If the graph lacks enough information, say so - do not hallucinate edges.
1045	
1046	```bash
1047	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1048	import sys, json
1049	from networkx.readwrite import json_graph
1050	import networkx as nx
1051	from pathlib import Path
1052	
1053	data = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/graph.json').read_text())
1054	G = json_graph.node_link_graph(data, edges='links')
1055	
1056	question = 'QUESTION'
1057	mode = 'MODE'  # 'bfs' or 'dfs'
1058	terms = [t.lower() for t in question.split() if len(t) > 3]
1059	
1060	# Find best-matching start nodes
1061	scored = []
1062	for nid, ndata in G.nodes(data=True):
1063	    label = ndata.get('label', '').lower()
1064	    score = sum(1 for t in terms if t in label)
1065	    if score > 0:
1066	        scored.append((score, nid))
1067	scored.sort(reverse=True)
1068	start_nodes = [nid for _, nid in scored[:3]]
1069	
1070	if not start_nodes:
1071	    print('No matching nodes found for query terms:', terms)
1072	    sys.exit(0)
1073	
1074	subgraph_nodes = set()
1075	subgraph_edges = []
1076	
1077	if mode == 'dfs':
1078	    # DFS: follow one path as deep as possible before backtracking.
1079	    # Depth-limited to 6 to avoid traversing the whole graph.
1080	    visited = set()
1081	    stack = [(n, 0) for n in reversed(start_nodes)]
1082	    while stack:
1083	        node, depth = stack.pop()
1084	        if node in visited or depth > 6:
1085	            continue
1086	        visited.add(node)
1087	        subgraph_nodes.add(node)
1088	        for neighbor in G.neighbors(node):
1089	            if neighbor not in visited:
1090	                stack.append((neighbor, depth + 1))
1091	                subgraph_edges.append((node, neighbor))
1092	else:
1093	    # BFS: explore all neighbors layer by layer up to depth 3.
1094	    frontier = set(start_nodes)
1095	    subgraph_nodes = set(start_nodes)
1096	    for _ in range(3):
1097	        next_frontier = set()
1098	        for n in frontier:
1099	            for neighbor in G.neighbors(n):
1100	                if neighbor not in subgraph_nodes:
1101	                    next_frontier.add(neighbor)
1102	                    subgraph_edges.append((n, neighbor))
1103	        subgraph_nodes.update(next_frontier)
1104	        frontier = next_frontier
1105	
1106	# Token-budget aware output: rank by relevance, cut at budget (~4 chars/token)
1107	token_budget = BUDGET  # default 2000
1108	char_budget = token_budget * 4
1109	
1110	# Score each node by term overlap for ranked output
1111	def relevance(nid):
1112	    label = G.nodes[nid].get('label', '').lower()
1113	    return sum(1 for t in terms if t in label)
1114	
1115	ranked_nodes = sorted(subgraph_nodes, key=relevance, reverse=True)
1116	
1117	lines = [f'Traversal: {mode.upper()} | Start: {[G.nodes[n].get(\"label\",n) for n in start_nodes]} | {len(subgraph_nodes)} nodes']
1118	for nid in ranked_nodes:
1119	    d = G.nodes[nid]
1120	    lines.append(f'  NODE {d.get(\"label\", nid)} [src={d.get(\"source_file\",\"\")} loc={d.get(\"source_location\",\"\")}]')
1121	for u, v in subgraph_edges:
1122	    if u in subgraph_nodes and v in subgraph_nodes:
1123	        d = G.edges[u, v]
1124	        lines.append(f'  EDGE {G.nodes[u].get(\"label\",u)} --{d.get(\"relation\",\"\")} [{d.get(\"confidence\",\"\")}]--> {G.nodes[v].get(\"label\",v)}')
1125	
1126	output = '\n'.join(lines)
1127	if len(output) > char_budget:
1128	    output = output[:char_budget] + f'\n... (truncated at ~{token_budget} token budget - use --budget N for more)'
1129	print(output)
1130	"
1131	```
1132	
1133	Replace `QUESTION` with the user's actual question, `MODE` with `bfs` or `dfs`, and `BUDGET` with the token budget (default `2000`, or whatever `--budget N` specifies). Then answer based on the subgraph output above.
1134	
1135	After writing the answer, save it back into the graph so it improves future queries:
1136	
1137	```bash
1138	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -m graphify save-result --question "QUESTION" --answer "ANSWER" --type query --nodes NODE1 NODE2
1139	```
1140	
1141	Replace `QUESTION` with the question, `ANSWER` with your full answer text, `SOURCE_NODES` with the list of node labels you cited. This closes the feedback loop: the next `--update` will extract this Q&A as a node in the graph.
1142	
1143	---
1144	
1145	## For /graphify path
1146	
1147	Find the shortest path between two named concepts in the graph.
1148	
1149	First check the graph exists:
1150	```bash
1151	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1152	from pathlib import Path
1153	if not Path('graphify-out/graph.json').exists():
1154	    print('ERROR: No graph found. Run /graphify <path> first to build the graph.')
1155	    raise SystemExit(1)
1156	"
1157	```
1158	If it fails, stop and tell the user to run `/graphify <path>` first.
1159	
1160	```bash
1161	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1162	import json, sys
1163	import networkx as nx
1164	from networkx.readwrite import json_graph
1165	from pathlib import Path
1166	
1167	data = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/graph.json').read_text())
1168	G = json_graph.node_link_graph(data, edges='links')
1169	
1170	a_term = 'NODE_A'
1171	b_term = 'NODE_B'
1172	
1173	def find_node(term):
1174	    term = term.lower()
1175	    scored = sorted(
1176	        [(sum(1 for w in term.split() if w in G.nodes[n].get('label','').lower()), n)
1177	         for n in G.nodes()],
1178	        reverse=True
1179	    )
1180	    return scored[0][1] if scored and scored[0][0] > 0 else None
1181	
1182	src = find_node(a_term)
1183	tgt = find_node(b_term)
1184	
1185	if not src or not tgt:
1186	    print(f'Could not find nodes matching: {a_term!r} or {b_term!r}')
1187	    sys.exit(0)
1188	
1189	try:
1190	    path = nx.shortest_path(G, src, tgt)
1191	    print(f'Shortest path ({len(path)-1} hops):')
1192	    for i, nid in enumerate(path):
1193	        label = G.nodes[nid].get('label', nid)
1194	        if i < len(path) - 1:
1195	            edge = G.edges[nid, path[i+1]]
1196	            rel = edge.get('relation', '')
1197	            conf = edge.get('confidence', '')
1198	            print(f'  {label} --{rel}--> [{conf}]')
1199	        else:
1200	            print(f'  {label}')
1201	except nx.NetworkXNoPath:
1202	    print(f'No path found between {a_term!r} and {b_term!r}')
1203	except nx.NodeNotFound as e:
1204	    print(f'Node not found: {e}')
1205	"
1206	```
1207	
1208	Replace `NODE_A` and `NODE_B` with the actual concept names from the user. Then explain the path in plain language - what each hop means, why it's significant.
1209	
1210	After writing the explanation, save it back:
1211	
1212	```bash
1213	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -m graphify save-result --question "Path from NODE_A to NODE_B" --answer "ANSWER" --type path_query --nodes NODE_A NODE_B
1214	```
1215	
1216	---
1217	
1218	## For /graphify explain
1219	
1220	Give a plain-language explanation of a single node - everything connected to it.
1221	
1222	First check the graph exists:
1223	```bash
1224	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1225	from pathlib import Path
1226	if not Path('graphify-out/graph.json').exists():
1227	    print('ERROR: No graph found. Run /graphify <path> first to build the graph.')
1228	    raise SystemExit(1)
1229	"
1230	```
1231	If it fails, stop and tell the user to run `/graphify <path>` first.
1232	
1233	```bash
1234	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1235	import json, sys
1236	import networkx as nx
1237	from networkx.readwrite import json_graph
1238	from pathlib import Path
1239	
1240	data = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/graph.json').read_text())
1241	G = json_graph.node_link_graph(data, edges='links')
1242	
1243	term = 'NODE_NAME'
1244	term_lower = term.lower()
1245	
1246	# Find best matching node
1247	scored = sorted(
1248	    [(sum(1 for w in term_lower.split() if w in G.nodes[n].get('label','').lower()), n)
1249	     for n in G.nodes()],
1250	    reverse=True
1251	)
1252	if not scored or scored[0][0] == 0:
1253	    print(f'No node matching {term!r}')
1254	    sys.exit(0)
1255	
1256	nid = scored[0][1]
1257	data_n = G.nodes[nid]
1258	print(f'NODE: {data_n.get(\"label\", nid)}')
1259	print(f'  source: {data_n.get(\"source_file\",\"unknown\")}')
1260	print(f'  type: {data_n.get(\"file_type\",\"unknown\")}')
1261	print(f'  degree: {G.degree(nid)}')
1262	print()
1263	print('CONNECTIONS:')
1264	for neighbor in G.neighbors(nid):
1265	    edge = G.edges[nid, neighbor]
1266	    nlabel = G.nodes[neighbor].get('label', neighbor)
1267	    rel = edge.get('relation', '')
1268	    conf = edge.get('confidence', '')
1269	    src_file = G.nodes[neighbor].get('source_file', '')
1270	    print(f'  --{rel}--> {nlabel} [{conf}] ({src_file})')
1271	"
1272	```
1273	
1274	Replace `NODE_NAME` with the concept the user asked about. Then write a 3-5 sentence explanation of what this node is, what it connects to, and why those connections are significant. Use the source locations as citations.
1275	
1276	After writing the explanation, save it back:
1277	
1278	```bash
1279	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -m graphify save-result --question "Explain NODE_NAME" --answer "ANSWER" --type explain --nodes NODE_NAME
1280	```
1281	
1282	---
1283	
1284	## For /graphify add
1285	
1286	Fetch a URL and add it to the corpus, then update the graph.
1287	
1288	```bash
1289	$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
1290	import sys
1291	from graphify.ingest import ingest
1292	from pathlib import Path
1293	
1294	try:
1295	    out = ingest('URL', Path('./raw'), author='AUTHOR', contributor='CONTRIBUTOR')
1296	    print(f'Saved to {out}')
1297	except ValueError as e:
1298	    print(f'error: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
1299	    sys.exit(1)
1300	except RuntimeError as e:
1301	    print(f'error: {e}', file=sys.stderr)
1302	    sys.exit(1)
1303	"
1304	```
1305	
1306	Replace `URL` with the actual URL, `AUTHOR` with the user's name if provided, `CONTRIBUTOR` likewise. If the command exits with an error, tell the user what went wrong - do not silently continue. After a successful save, automatically run the `--update` pipeline on `./raw` to merge the new file into the existing graph.
1307	
1308	Supported URL types (auto-detected):
1309	- YouTube / any video URL → audio downloaded via yt-dlp, transcribed to `.txt` on next run (requires `pip install 'graphifyy[video]'`)
1310	- Twitter/X → fetched via oEmbed, saved as `.md` with tweet text and author
1311	- arXiv → abstract + metadata saved as `.md`
1312	- PDF → downloaded as `.pdf`
1313	- Images (.png/.jpg/.webp) → downloaded, Claude vision extracts on next run
1314	- Any webpage → converted to markdown via html2text
1315	
1316	---
1317	
1318	## For --watch
1319	
1320	Start a background watcher that monitors a folder and auto-updates the graph when files change.
1321	
1322	```bash
1323	python3 -m graphify.watch INPUT_PATH --debounce 3
1324	```
1325	
1326	Replace INPUT_PATH with the folder to watch. Behavior depends on what changed:
1327	
1328	- **Code files only (.py, .ts, .go, etc.):** re-runs AST extraction + rebuild + cluster immediately, no LLM needed. `graph.json` and `GRAPH_REPORT.md` are updated automatically.
1329	- **Docs, papers, or images:** writes a `graphify-out/needs_update` flag and prints a notification to run `/graphify --update` (LLM semantic re-extraction required).
1330	
1331	Debounce (default 3s): waits until file activity stops before triggering, so a wave of parallel agent writes doesn't trigger a rebuild per file.
1332	
1333	Press Ctrl+C to stop.
1334	
1335	For agentic workflows: run `--watch` in a background terminal. Code changes from agent waves are picked up automatically between waves. If agents are also writing docs or notes, you'll need a manual `/graphify --update` after those waves.
1336	
1337	---
1338	
1339	## For git commit hook
1340	
1341	Install a post-commit hook that auto-rebuilds the graph after every commit. No background process needed - triggers once per commit, works with any editor.
1342	
1343	```bash
1344	graphify hook install    # install
1345	graphify hook uninstall  # remove
1346	graphify hook status     # check
1347	```
1348	
1349	After every `git commit`, the hook detects which code files changed (via `git diff HEAD~1`), re-runs AST extraction on those files, and rebuilds `graph.json` and `GRAPH_REPORT.md`. Doc/image changes are ignored by the hook - run `/graphify --update` manually for those.
1350	
1351	If a post-commit hook already exists, graphify appends to it rather than replacing it.
1352	
1353	---
1354	
1355	## For native CLAUDE.md integration
1356	
1357	Run once per project to make graphify always-on in Claude Code sessions:
1358	
1359	```bash
1360	graphify claude install
1361	```
1362	
1363	This writes a `## graphify` section to the local `CLAUDE.md` that instructs Claude to check the graph before answering codebase questions and rebuild it after code changes. No manual `/graphify` needed in future sessions.
1364	
1365	```bash
1366	graphify claude uninstall  # remove the section
1367	```
1368	
1369	---
1370	
1371	## Honesty Rules
1372	
1373	- Never invent an edge. If unsure, use AMBIGUOUS.
1374	- Never skip the corpus check warning.
1375	- Always show token cost in the report.
1376	- Never hide cohesion scores behind symbols - show the raw number.
1377	- Never run HTML viz on a graph with more than 5,000 nodes without warning the user.
1378

file.content:
---
name: graphify
description: "any input (code, docs, papers, images) - knowledge graph - clustered communities - HTML + JSON + audit report"
trigger: /graphify
---

# /graphify

Turn any folder of files into a navigable knowledge graph with community detection, an honest audit trail, and three outputs: interactive HTML, GraphRAG-ready JSON, and a plain-language GRAPH_REPORT.md.

## Usage

```
/graphify                                             # full pipeline on current directory → Obsidian vault
/graphify <path>                                      # full pipeline on specific path
/graphify https://github.com/<owner>/<repo>           # clone repo then run full pipeline on it
/graphify https://github.com/<owner>/<repo> --branch <branch>  # clone a specific branch
/graphify <url1> <url2> ...                           # clone multiple repos, build each, merge into one cross-repo graph
/graphify <path> --mode deep                          # thorough extraction, richer INFERRED edges
/graphify <path> --update                             # incremental - re-extract only new/changed files
/graphify <path> --directed                            # build directed graph (preserves edge direction: source→target)
/graphify <path> --whisper-model medium                # use a larger Whisper model for better transcription accuracy
/graphify <path> --cluster-only                       # rerun clustering on existing graph
/graphify <path> --no-viz                             # skip visualization, just report + JSON
/graphify <path> --html                               # (HTML is generated by default - this flag is a no-op)
/graphify <path> --svg                                # also export graph.svg (embeds in Notion, GitHub)
/graphify <path> --graphml                            # export graph.graphml (Gephi, yEd)
/graphify <path> --neo4j                              # generate graphify-out/cypher.txt for Neo4j
/graphify <path> --neo4j-push bolt://localhost:7687   # push directly to Neo4j
/graphify <path> --mcp                                # start MCP stdio server for agent access
/graphify <path> --watch                              # watch folder, auto-rebuild on code changes (no LLM needed)
/graphify <path> --wiki                               # build agent-crawlable wiki (index.md + one article per community)
/graphify <path> --obsidian --obsidian-dir ~/vaults/my-project  # write vault to custom path (e.g. existing vault)
/graphify add <url>                                   # fetch URL, save to ./raw, update graph
/graphify add <url> --author "Name"                   # tag who wrote it
/graphify add <url> --contributor "Name"              # tag who added it to the corpus
/graphify query "<question>"                          # BFS traversal - broad context
/graphify query "<question>" --dfs                    # DFS - trace a specific path
/graphify query "<question>" --budget 1500            # cap answer at N tokens
/graphify path "AuthModule" "Database"                # shortest path between two concepts
/graphify explain "SwinTransformer"                   # plain-language explanation of a node
```

## What graphify is for

graphify is built around Andrej Karpathy's /raw folder workflow: drop anything into a folder - papers, tweets, screenshots, code, notes - and get a structured knowledge graph that shows you what you didn't know was connected.

Three things it does that Claude alone cannot:
1. **Persistent graph** - relationships are stored in `graphify-out/graph.json` and survive across sessions. Ask questions weeks later without re-reading everything.
2. **Honest audit trail** - every edge is tagged EXTRACTED, INFERRED, or AMBIGUOUS. You know what was found vs invented.
3. **Cross-document surprise** - community detection finds connections between concepts in different files that you would never think to ask about directly.

Use it for:
- A codebase you're new to (understand architecture before touching anything)
- A reading list (papers + tweets + notes → one navigable graph)
- A research corpus (citation graph + concept graph in one)
- Your personal /raw folder (drop everything in, let it grow, query it)

## What You Must Do When Invoked

If no path was given, use `.` (current directory). Do not ask the user for a path.

If the path argument starts with `https://github.com/` or `http://github.com/`, treat it as a GitHub URL — run Step 0 before anything else, then continue with the resolved local path.

Follow these steps in order. Do not skip steps.

### Step 0 - Clone GitHub repo(s) (only if a GitHub URL was given)

**Single repo:**
```bash
LOCAL_PATH=$(graphify clone <github-url> [--branch <branch>])
# Use LOCAL_PATH as the target for all subsequent steps
```

**Multiple repos (cross-repo graph):**
```bash
# Clone each repo, run the full pipeline on each, then merge
graphify clone <url1>   # → ~/.graphify/repos/<owner1>/<repo1>
graphify clone <url2>   # → ~/.graphify/repos/<owner2>/<repo2>
# Run /graphify on each local path to produce their graph.json files
# Then merge:
graphify merge-graphs \
  ~/.graphify/repos/<owner1>/<repo1>/graphify-out/graph.json \
  ~/.graphify/repos/<owner2>/<repo2>/graphify-out/graph.json \
  --out graphify-out/cross-repo-graph.json
```

Graphify clones into `~/.graphify/repos/<owner>/<repo>` and reuses existing clones on repeat runs. Each node in the merged graph carries a `repo` attribute so you can filter by origin.

### Step 1 - Ensure graphify is installed

```bash
# Detect the correct Python interpreter (handles uv tool, pipx, venv, system installs)
PYTHON=""
GRAPHIFY_BIN=$(which graphify 2>/dev/null)
# 1. uv tool installs — most reliable on modern Mac/Linux
if [ -z "$PYTHON" ] && command -v uv >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    _UV_PY=$(uv tool run graphifyy python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)" 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$_UV_PY" ]; then PYTHON="$_UV_PY"; fi
fi
# 2. Read shebang from graphify binary (pipx and direct pip installs)
if [ -z "$PYTHON" ] && [ -n "$GRAPHIFY_BIN" ]; then
    _SHEBANG=$(head -1 "$GRAPHIFY_BIN" | tr -d '#!')
    case "$_SHEBANG" in
        *[!a-zA-Z0-9/_.-]*) ;;
        *) "$_SHEBANG" -c "import graphify" 2>/dev/null && PYTHON="$_SHEBANG" ;;
    esac
fi
# 3. Fall back to python3
if [ -z "$PYTHON" ]; then PYTHON="python3"; fi
"$PYTHON" -c "import graphify" 2>/dev/null || "$PYTHON" -m pip install graphifyy -q 2>/dev/null || "$PYTHON" -m pip install graphifyy -q --break-system-packages 2>&1 | tail -3
# Write interpreter path for all subsequent steps (persists across invocations)
mkdir -p graphify-out
"$PYTHON" -c "import sys; open('graphify-out/.graphify_python', 'w').write(sys.executable)"
```

If the import succeeds, print nothing and move straight to Step 2.

**In every subsequent bash block, replace `python3` with `$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python)` to use the correct interpreter.**

### Step 2 - Detect files

```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import json
from graphify.detect import detect
from pathlib import Path
result = detect(Path('INPUT_PATH'))
print(json.dumps(result))
" > graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json
```

Replace INPUT_PATH with the actual path the user provided. Do NOT cat or print the JSON - read it silently and present a clean summary instead:

```
Corpus: X files · ~Y words
  code:     N files (.py .ts .go ...)
  docs:     N files (.md .txt ...)
  papers:   N files (.pdf ...)
  images:   N files
  video:    N files (.mp4 .mp3 ...)
```

Omit any category with 0 files from the summary.

Then act on it:
- If `total_files` is 0: stop with "No supported files found in [path]."
- If `skipped_sensitive` is non-empty: mention file count skipped, not the file names.
- If `total_words` > 2,000,000 OR `total_files` > 200: show the warning and the top 5 subdirectories by file count, then ask which subfolder to run on. Wait for the user's answer before proceeding.
- Otherwise: proceed directly to Step 2.5 if video files were detected, or Step 3 if not.

### Step 2.5 - Transcribe video / audio files (only if video files detected)

Skip this step entirely if `detect` returned zero `video` files.

Video and audio files cannot be read directly. Transcribe them to text first, then treat the transcripts as doc files in Step 3.

**Strategy:** Read the god nodes from `graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json` (or the analysis file if it exists from a previous run). You are already a language model — write a one-sentence domain hint yourself from those labels. Then pass it to Whisper as the initial prompt. No separate API call needed.

**However**, if the corpus has *only* video files and no other docs/code, use the generic fallback prompt: `"Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks."`

**Step 1 - Write the Whisper prompt yourself.**

Read the top god node labels from detect output or analysis, then compose a short domain hint sentence, for example:

- Labels: `transformer, attention, encoder, decoder` → `"Machine learning research on transformer architectures and attention mechanisms. Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks."`
- Labels: `kubernetes, deployment, pod, helm` → `"DevOps discussion about Kubernetes deployments and Helm charts. Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks."`

Set it as `WHISPER_PROMPT` to use in the next command.

**Step 2 - Transcribe:**

```bash
GRAPHIFY_WHISPER_MODEL=base  # or whatever --whisper-model the user passed
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import json, os
from pathlib import Path
from graphify.transcribe import transcribe_all

detect = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())
video_files = detect.get('files', {}).get('video', [])
prompt = os.environ.get('GRAPHIFY_WHISPER_PROMPT', 'Use proper punctuation and paragraph breaks.')

transcript_paths = transcribe_all(video_files, initial_prompt=prompt)
print(json.dumps(transcript_paths))
" > graphify-out/.graphify_transcripts.json
```

After transcription:
- Read the transcript paths from `graphify-out/.graphify_transcripts.json`
- Add them to the docs list before dispatching semantic subagents in Step 3B
- Print how many transcripts were created: `Transcribed N video file(s) -> treating as docs`
- If transcription fails for a file, print a warning and continue with the rest

**Whisper model:** Default is `base`. If the user passed `--whisper-model <name>`, set `GRAPHIFY_WHISPER_MODEL=<name>` in the environment before running the command above.

### Step 3 - Extract entities and relationships

**Before starting:** note whether `--mode deep` was given. You must pass `DEEP_MODE=true` to every subagent in Step B2 if it was. Track this from the original invocation - do not lose it.

This step has two parts: **structural extraction** (deterministic, free) and **semantic extraction** (Claude, costs tokens).

**Run Part A (AST) and Part B (semantic) in parallel. Dispatch all semantic subagents AND start AST extraction in the same message. Both can run simultaneously since they operate on different file types. Merge results in Part C as before.**

Note: Parallelizing AST + semantic saves 5-15s on large corpora. AST is deterministic and fast; start it while subagents are processing docs/papers.

#### Part A - Structural extraction for code files

For any code files detected, run AST extraction in parallel with Part B subagents:

```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import sys, json
from graphify.extract import collect_files, extract
from pathlib import Path
import json

code_files = []
detect = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())
for f in detect.get('files', {}).get('code', []):
    code_files.extend(collect_files(Path(f)) if Path(f).is_dir() else [Path(f)])

if code_files:
    result = extract(code_files, cache_root=Path('.'))
    Path('graphify-out/.graphify_ast.json').write_text(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
    print(f'AST: {len(result[\"nodes\"])} nodes, {len(result[\"edges\"])} edges')
else:
    Path('graphify-out/.graphify_ast.json').write_text(json.dumps({'nodes':[],'edges':[],'input_tokens':0,'output_tokens':0}))
    print('No code files - skipping AST extraction')
"
```

#### Part B - Semantic extraction (parallel subagents)

**Fast path:** If detection found zero docs, papers, and images (code-only corpus), skip Part B entirely and go straight to Part C. AST handles code - there is nothing for semantic subagents to do.

**MANDATORY: You MUST use the Agent tool here. Reading files yourself one-by-one is forbidden - it is 5-10x slower. If you do not use the Agent tool you are doing this wrong.**

Before dispatching subagents, print a timing estimate:
- Load `total_words` and file counts from `graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json`
- Estimate agents needed: `ceil(uncached_non_code_files / 22)` (chunk size is 20-25)
- Estimate time: ~45s per agent batch (they run in parallel, so total ≈ 45s × ceil(agents/parallel_limit))
- Print: "Semantic extraction: ~N files → X agents, estimated ~Ys"

**Step B0 - Check extraction cache first**

Before dispatching any subagents, check which files already have cached extraction results:

```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import json
from graphify.cache import check_semantic_cache
from pathlib import Path

detect = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())
all_files = [f for files in detect['files'].values() for f in files]

cached_nodes, cached_edges, cached_hyperedges, uncached = check_semantic_cache(all_files)

if cached_nodes or cached_edges or cached_hyperedges:
    Path('graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json').write_text(json.dumps({'nodes': cached_nodes, 'edges': cached_edges, 'hyperedges': cached_hyperedges}))
Path('graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt').write_text('\n'.join(uncached))
print(f'Cache: {len(all_files)-len(uncached)} files hit, {len(uncached)} files need extraction')
"
```

Only dispatch subagents for files listed in `graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt`. If all files are cached, skip to Part C directly.

**Step B1 - Split into chunks**

Load files from `graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt`. Split into chunks of 20-25 files each. Each image gets its own chunk (vision needs separate context). When splitting, group files from the same directory together so related artifacts land in the same chunk and cross-file relationships are more likely to be extracted.

**Step B2 - Dispatch ALL subagents in a single message**

Call the Agent tool multiple times IN THE SAME RESPONSE - one call per chunk. This is the only way they run in parallel. If you make one Agent call, wait, then make another, you are doing it sequentially and defeating the purpose.

**IMPORTANT - subagent type:** Always use `subagent_type="general-purpose"`. Do NOT use `Explore` - it is read-only and cannot write chunk files to disk, which silently drops extraction results. General-purpose has Write and Bash access which the subagent needs.

Concrete example for 3 chunks:
```
[Agent tool call 1: files 1-15, subagent_type="general-purpose"]
[Agent tool call 2: files 16-30, subagent_type="general-purpose"]
[Agent tool call 3: files 31-45, subagent_type="general-purpose"]
```
All three in one message. Not three separate messages.

Each subagent receives this exact prompt (substitute FILE_LIST, CHUNK_NUM, TOTAL_CHUNKS, and DEEP_MODE):

```
You are a graphify extraction subagent. Read the files listed and extract a knowledge graph fragment.
Output ONLY valid JSON matching the schema below - no explanation, no markdown fences, no preamble.

Files (chunk CHUNK_NUM of TOTAL_CHUNKS):
FILE_LIST

Rules:
- EXTRACTED: relationship explicit in source (import, call, citation, "see §3.2")
- INFERRED: reasonable inference (shared data structure, implied dependency)
- AMBIGUOUS: uncertain - flag for review, do not omit

Code files: focus on semantic edges AST cannot find (call relationships, shared data, arch patterns).
  Do not re-extract imports - AST already has those.
Doc/paper files: extract named concepts, entities, citations. Also extract rationale — sections that explain WHY a decision was made, trade-offs chosen, or design intent. These become nodes with `rationale_for` edges pointing to the concept they explain.
Image files: use vision to understand what the image IS - do not just OCR.
  UI screenshot: layout patterns, design decisions, key elements, purpose.
  Chart: metric, trend/insight, data source.
  Tweet/post: claim as node, author, concepts mentioned.
  Diagram: components and connections.
  Research figure: what it demonstrates, method, result.
  Handwritten/whiteboard: ideas and arrows, mark uncertain readings AMBIGUOUS.

DEEP_MODE (if --mode deep was given): be aggressive with INFERRED edges - indirect deps,
  shared assumptions, latent couplings. Mark uncertain ones AMBIGUOUS instead of omitting.

Semantic similarity: if two concepts in this chunk solve the same problem or represent the same idea without any structural link (no import, no call, no citation), add a `semantically_similar_to` edge marked INFERRED with a confidence_score reflecting how similar they are (0.6-0.95). Examples:
- Two functions that both validate user input but never call each other
- A class in code and a concept in a paper that describe the same algorithm
- Two error types that handle the same failure mode differently
Only add these when the similarity is genuinely non-obvious and cross-cutting. Do not add them for trivially similar things.

Hyperedges: if 3 or more nodes clearly participate together in a shared concept, flow, or pattern that is not captured by pairwise edges alone, add a hyperedge to a top-level `hyperedges` array. Examples:
- All classes that implement a common protocol or interface
- All functions in an authentication flow (even if they don't all call each other)
- All concepts from a paper section that form one coherent idea
Use sparingly — only when the group relationship adds information beyond the pairwise edges. Maximum 3 hyperedges per chunk.

If a file has YAML frontmatter (--- ... ---), copy source_url, captured_at, author,
  contributor onto every node from that file.

confidence_score is REQUIRED on every edge - never omit it, never use 0.5 as a default:
- EXTRACTED edges: confidence_score = 1.0 always
- INFERRED edges: reason about each edge individually.
  Direct structural evidence (shared data structure, clear dependency): 0.8-0.9.
  Reasonable inference with some uncertainty: 0.6-0.7.
  Weak or speculative: 0.4-0.5. Most edges should be 0.6-0.9, not 0.5.
- AMBIGUOUS edges: 0.1-0.3

Node ID format: lowercase, only `[a-z0-9_]`, no dots or slashes. Format: `{stem}_{entity}` where stem is the filename without extension and entity is the symbol name, both normalized (lowercase, non-alphanumeric chars replaced with `_`). Example: `src/auth/session.py` + `ValidateToken` → `session_validatetoken`. This must match the ID the AST extractor generates so cross-references between code and semantic nodes connect correctly. CRITICAL: never append chunk numbers, sequence numbers, or any suffix to an ID (no `_c1`, `_c2`, `_chunk2`, etc.). IDs must be deterministic from the label alone — the same entity must always produce the same ID regardless of which chunk processes it.

Output exactly this JSON (no other text):
{"nodes":[{"id":"session_validatetoken","label":"Human Readable Name","file_type":"code|document|paper|image","source_file":"relative/path","source_location":null,"source_url":null,"captured_at":null,"author":null,"contributor":null}],"edges":[{"source":"node_id","target":"node_id","relation":"calls|implements|references|cites|conceptually_related_to|shares_data_with|semantically_similar_to|rationale_for","confidence":"EXTRACTED|INFERRED|AMBIGUOUS","confidence_score":1.0,"source_file":"relative/path","source_location":null,"weight":1.0}],"hyperedges":[{"id":"snake_case_id","label":"Human Readable Label","nodes":["node_id1","node_id2","node_id3"],"relation":"participate_in|implement|form","confidence":"EXTRACTED|INFERRED","confidence_score":0.75,"source_file":"relative/path"}],"input_tokens":0,"output_tokens":0}
```

**Step B3 - Collect, cache, and merge**

Wait for all subagents. For each result:
- Check that `graphify-out/.graphify_chunk_NN.json` exists on disk — this is the success signal
- If the file exists and contains valid JSON with `nodes` and `edges`, include it and save to cache
- If the file is missing, the subagent was likely dispatched as read-only (Explore type) — print a warning: "chunk N missing from disk — subagent may have been read-only. Re-run with general-purpose agent." Do not silently skip.
- If a subagent failed or returned invalid JSON, print a warning and skip that chunk - do not abort

If more than half the chunks failed or are missing, stop and tell the user to re-run and ensure `subagent_type="general-purpose"` is used.

Save new results to cache:
```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import json
from graphify.cache import save_semantic_cache
from pathlib import Path

new = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').exists() else {'nodes':[],'edges':[],'hyperedges':[]}
saved = save_semantic_cache(new.get('nodes', []), new.get('edges', []), new.get('hyperedges', []))
print(f'Cached {saved} files')
"
```

Merge cached + new results into `graphify-out/.graphify_semantic.json`:
```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import json
from pathlib import Path

cached = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json').exists() else {'nodes':[],'edges':[],'hyperedges':[]}
new = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json').exists() else {'nodes':[],'edges':[],'hyperedges':[]}

all_nodes = cached['nodes'] + new.get('nodes', [])
all_edges = cached['edges'] + new.get('edges', [])
all_hyperedges = cached.get('hyperedges', []) + new.get('hyperedges', [])
seen = set()
deduped = []
for n in all_nodes:
    if n['id'] not in seen:
        seen.add(n['id'])
        deduped.append(n)

merged = {
    'nodes': deduped,
    'edges': all_edges,
    'hyperedges': all_hyperedges,
    'input_tokens': new.get('input_tokens', 0),
    'output_tokens': new.get('output_tokens', 0),
}
Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic.json').write_text(json.dumps(merged, indent=2))
print(f'Extraction complete - {len(deduped)} nodes, {len(all_edges)} edges ({len(cached[\"nodes\"])} from cache, {len(new.get(\"nodes\",[]))} new)')
"
```
Clean up temp files: `rm -f graphify-out/.graphify_cached.json graphify-out/.graphify_uncached.txt graphify-out/.graphify_semantic_new.json`

#### Part C - Merge AST + semantic into final extraction

```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import sys, json
from pathlib import Path

ast = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_ast.json').read_text())
sem = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_semantic.json').read_text())

# Merge: AST nodes first, semantic nodes deduplicated by id
seen = {n['id'] for n in ast['nodes']}
merged_nodes = list(ast['nodes'])
for n in sem['nodes']:
    if n['id'] not in seen:
        merged_nodes.append(n)
        seen.add(n['id'])

merged_edges = ast['edges'] + sem['edges']
merged_hyperedges = sem.get('hyperedges', [])
merged = {
    'nodes': merged_nodes,
    'edges': merged_edges,
    'hyperedges': merged_hyperedges,
    'input_tokens': sem.get('input_tokens', 0),
    'output_tokens': sem.get('output_tokens', 0),
}
Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').write_text(json.dumps(merged, indent=2))
total = len(merged_nodes)
edges = len(merged_edges)
print(f'Merged: {total} nodes, {edges} edges ({len(ast[\"nodes\"])} AST + {len(sem[\"nodes\"])} semantic)')
"
```

### Step 4 - Build graph, cluster, analyze, generate outputs

**Before starting:** note whether `--directed` was given. If so, pass `directed=True` to `build_from_json()` in the code block below. This builds a `DiGraph` that preserves edge direction (source→target) instead of the default undirected `Graph`.

```bash
mkdir -p graphify-out
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import sys, json
from graphify.build import build_from_json
from graphify.cluster import cluster, score_all
from graphify.analyze import god_nodes, surprising_connections, suggest_questions
from graphify.report import generate
from graphify.export import to_json
from pathlib import Path

extraction = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').read_text())
detection  = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_detect.json').read_text())

G = build_from_json(extraction)
communities = cluster(G)
cohesion = score_all(G, communities)
tokens = {'input': extraction.get('input_tokens', 0), 'output': extraction.get('output_tokens', 0)}
gods = god_nodes(G)
surprises = surprising_connections(G, communities)
labels = {cid: 'Community ' + str(cid) for cid in communities}
# Placeholder questions - regenerated with real labels in Step 5
questions = suggest_questions(G, communities, labels)

report = generate(G, communities, cohesion, labels, gods, surprises, detection, tokens, 'INPUT_PATH', suggested_questions=questions)
Path('graphify-out/GRAPH_REPORT.md').write_text(report)
to_json(G, communities, 'graphify-out/graph.json')

analysis = {
    'communities': {str(k): v for k, v in communities.items()},
    'cohesion': {str(k): v for k, v in cohesion.items()},
    'gods': gods,
    'surprises': surprises,
    'questions': questions,
}
Path('graphify-out/.graphify_analysis.json').write_text(json.dumps(analysis, indent=2))
if G.number_of_nodes() == 0:
    print('ERROR: Graph is empty - extraction produced no nodes.')
    print('Possible causes: all files were skipped, binary-only corpus, or extraction failed.')
    raise SystemExit(1)
print(f'Graph: {G.number_of_nodes()} nodes, {G.number_of_edges()} edges, {len(communities)} communities')
"
```

If this step prints `ERROR: Graph is empty`, stop and tell the user what happened - do not proceed to labeling or visualization.

Replace INPUT_PATH with the actual path.

### Step 6 - Generate Obsidian vault (opt-in) + HTML

**Generate HTML always** (unless `--no-viz`). **Obsidian vault only if `--obsidian` was explicitly given** — skip it otherwise, it generates one file per node.

If `--obsidian` was given:

- If `--obsidian-dir <path>` was also given, use that path as the vault directory. Otherwise default to `graphify-out/obsidian`.

```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import sys, json
from graphify.build import build_from_json
from graphify.export import to_obsidian, to_canvas
from pathlib import Path

extraction = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').read_text())
analysis   = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_analysis.json').read_text())
labels_raw = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_labels.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_labels.json').exists() else {}

G = build_from_json(extraction)
communities = {int(k): v for k, v in analysis['communities'].items()}
cohesion = {int(k): v for k, v in analysis['cohesion'].items()}
labels = {int(k): v for k, v in labels_raw.items()}

obsidian_dir = 'OBSIDIAN_DIR'  # replace with --obsidian-dir value, or 'graphify-out/obsidian' if not given

n = to_obsidian(G, communities, obsidian_dir, community_labels=labels or None, cohesion=cohesion)
print(f'Obsidian vault: {n} notes in {obsidian_dir}/')

to_canvas(G, communities, f'{obsidian_dir}/graph.canvas', community_labels=labels or None)
print(f'Canvas: {obsidian_dir}/graph.canvas - open in Obsidian for structured community layout')
print()
print(f'Open {obsidian_dir}/ as a vault in Obsidian.')
print('  Graph view   - nodes colored by community (set automatically)')
print('  graph.canvas - structured layout with communities as groups')
print('  _COMMUNITY_* - overview notes with cohesion scores and dataview queries')

Generate the HTML graph (always, unless `--no-viz`):

```bash
$(cat graphify-out/.graphify_python) -c "
import sys, json
from graphify.build import build_from_json
from graphify.export import to_html
from pathlib import Path

extraction = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_extract.json').read_text())
analysis   = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_analysis.json').read_text())
labels_raw = json.loads(Path('graphify-out/.graphify_labels.json').read_text()) if Path('graphify-out/.graphify_labels.json').exists() else {}

G = build_from_json(extraction)
communities = {int(k): v for k, v in analysis['communities'].items()}
labels = {int(k): v for k, v in labels_raw.items()}

NODE_LIMIT = 5000
if G.number_of_nodes() > NODE_LIMIT:
    from collections import Counter
    print(f'Graph has {G.number_of_nodes()} nodes (above {NODE_LIMIT} limit). Building aggregated community view...')
    node_to_community = {nid: cid for cid, members in communities.items() for nid in members}
    import networkx as nx_meta
    meta = nx_meta.Graph()
    for cid, members in communities.items():
        meta.add_node(str(cid), label=labels.get(cid, f'Community {cid}'))
    edge_counts = Counter()
    for u, v in G.edges():
        cu, cv = node_to_community.get(u), node_to_community.get(v)
        if cu is not None and cv is not None and cu != cv:
            edge_counts[(min(cu, cv), max(cu, cv))] += 1
    for (cu, cv), w in edge_counts.items():
        meta.add_edge(str(cu), str(cv), weight=w, relation=f'{w} cross-community edges', confidence='AGGREGATED')
    if meta.number_of_nodes() > 1:
        meta_communities = {cid: [str(cid)] for cid in communities}
        member_counts = {cid: len(members) for cid, members in communities.items()}
        to_html(meta, meta_communities, 'graphify-out/graph.html', community_labels=labels or None, member_counts=member_counts)
        print(f'graph.html written (aggregated: {meta.number_of_nodes()} community nodes, {meta.number_of_edges()} cross-community edges)')
        print('Tip: run with --obsidian for full node-level detail.')
    else:
        print('Single community — aggregated view not useful. Skipping graph.html.')
else:
    to_html(G, communities, 'graphify-out/graph.html', community_labels=labels or None)
    print('graph.html written - open in any browser, no server needed')
"
```

## Interpreter guard for subcommands

Before running any subcommand below (`--update`, `--cluster-only`, `query`, `path`, `explain`, `add`), check that `.graphify_python` exists. If it's missing (e.g. user deleted `graphify-out/`), re-resolve the interpreter first:

```bash
if [ ! -f graphify-out/.graphify_python ]; then
    GRAPHIFY_BIN=$(which graphify 2>/dev/null)
    if [ -n "$GRAPHIFY_BIN" ]; then
        PYTHON=$(head -1 "$GRAPHIFY_BIN" | tr -d '#!')
        case "$PYTHON" in *[!a-zA-Z0-9/_.-]*) PYTHON="python3" ;; esac
    else
        PYTHON="python3"
    fi
    mkdir -p graphify-out
    "$PYTHON" -c "import sys; open('graphify-out/.graphify_python', 'w').write(sys.executable)"
fi
```
